verdict_reasoning: C3 is a soluble plasma protein that undergoes proteolytic cleavage upon complement activation, generating C3b. The C3b fragment becomes stably anchored to pathogen and complement-activated cell surfaces via covalent thioester bonding—a wash-resistant mechanism. C3 reaches the outer membrane through this stable post-translational covalent attachment of its cleavage product, not through an intrinsic membrane anchor. The thioester-mediated deposition is explicitly documented and represents a canonical complement-activation surface attachment.
